title: Login redirect loop after SSO change
body:
We recently switched our company's SSO provider from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Since the switch, some users are getting stuck in an infinite redirect loop when trying to log into TaskFlow. They click login, get redirected to Microsoft, authenticate successfully, get redirected back to TaskFlow, and then immediately get sent back to Microsoft again. Not all users are affected — maybe 30% of our team. The rest can log in fine.

We've already verified that the Entra ID app registration is correct and matches our redirect URIs. The affected users can log into other apps that use the same SSO just fine.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: Thanks for the detailed report — that redirect loop sounds frustrating. One thing that stands out is that only ~30% of users are affected. Have you been able to identify any pattern that distinguishes the affected users from those who can log in fine? For example: were they migrated differently in Entra ID, do they belong to different groups or have different role assignments, or have you noticed whether it correlates with anything like browser choice, whether they had existing active sessions, or how their accounts were provisioned?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question. I've been trying to figure out a pattern myself. It doesn't seem to be browser-related — I've seen it happen on Chrome, Edge, and Firefox. One thing I noticed is that several of the affected users have those plus-sign email addresses, like jane+taskflow@company.com. We use that format for some people so they can filter TaskFlow notifications. But I'm not 100% sure that accounts for all the affected users. There might also be a few people whose email addresses changed at some point — like when we set up Entra we mapped some people to slightly different primary emails than what they had in Okta. I haven't done a full audit though. The users who work fine seem to just have straightforward email addresses that are the same in both systems.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: That's a really useful observation about the plus-sign addresses and the email mapping differences. To help narrow this down further: when the redirect loop happens, are you able to capture what's in the OIDC token coming back from Entra ID — specifically the email or subject claim? Comparing that to what TaskFlow has stored for an affected user would tell us whether TaskFlow is failing to match the identity and forcing re-authentication. If checking the token isn't easy, another angle: could you pick one affected user and temporarily change their Entra ID primary email to exactly match what they had in Okta, and see if that breaks the loop? That would confirm whether the email mismatch is the root cause.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I'm not really sure how to look at the OIDC token — that's a bit over my head. I could probably figure it out if you pointed me to instructions, but I haven't done that before. I did try something similar to your second suggestion though — for one user who had a different email mapped in Entra, I updated their Entra primary email to match what was in Okta, and... I think the redirect loop still happened actually. So maybe it's not just the email mismatch? Or maybe I needed to clear something on TaskFlow's side too. I did notice that one of the affected users managed to get in once by using an incognito window, which was weird. But when they tried again in their normal browser it went back to the loop. I'm not sure what to make of that.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That incognito window detail is actually a really important clue — it suggests the problem might be stale authentication cookies or cached session data left over from the Okta setup, rather than (or in addition to) the email differences.

Could you try something with one of the affected users? Have them clear their browser cookies specifically for your TaskFlow domain (and your Entra ID / login.microsoftonline.com domain), then attempt to log in again in their normal browser. If that breaks the loop, it would confirm that leftover session state is the culprit.

Also — does TaskFlow store its own session cookies or tokens (like a JWT in localStorage or a session cookie)? If so, the app might be holding onto a session tied to the old Okta identity and repeatedly failing to validate it against Entra, which would explain the loop regardless of email correctness.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Yeah, I actually already had a couple people try clearing their cookies for TaskFlow and it did seem to help temporarily — they could get in right after clearing everything. But then the next time they closed the browser and came back, or sometimes even if they just let it sit for a while, the loop came back. So it's like it fixes itself for a moment but then breaks again.

As for TaskFlow's session storage — I'm not a developer so I'm not sure about the technical details, but I know it uses cookies because I can see them in the browser settings when I look. I did poke around in the browser dev tools at one point and I could see a Set-Cookie header in the redirect response coming back from Microsoft, but honestly I wasn't sure what I was looking at. I don't know if it uses JWTs or localStorage or anything like that — I'd have to ask our dev team to look into that side of things.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks — the fact that clearing cookies works temporarily but the loop comes back is really telling. That means something in the login flow is re-creating the bad state each time.

I want to try to nail down the pattern before handing this off to the dev team. Could you do a quick check: of the affected users, how many have plus-sign email addresses (like `jane+taskflow@company.com`) versus how many just had their email mapped differently during the Entra migration? And on the flip side — are there any users with plus-sign addresses who are *not* experiencing the loop? That distinction will make a big difference in where the developers start looking.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Let me think... Off the top of my head, I'd say maybe 4 or 5 of the affected users have the plus-sign emails. Then there are probably another 3 or 4 who had their emails remapped during the Entra migration. There might be some overlap — I'm not totally sure without pulling up a spreadsheet. As for plus-sign users who are NOT affected... I don't think we have that many plus-sign addresses total, maybe 6 or 7 people? So if 4 or 5 of them are having issues, most of them are affected but maybe one or two aren't. I'd have to double-check though, I'm going off memory here.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Infinite login redirect loop after Okta-to-Entra-ID SSO migration for users with plus-sign or remapped email addresses","problem":"After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop: they authenticate successfully at Entra ID, get redirected back to TaskFlow, and are immediately redirected back to Entra ID. Clearing cookies temporarily fixes the issue, but the loop recurs on subsequent visits or after idle time, indicating the login flow itself is re-creating the bad state.","root_cause_hypothesis":"TaskFlow's authentication callback is likely failing to match the identity from Entra ID's OIDC token to the existing user record, due to email normalization issues (plus-sign addresses like jane+taskflow@company.com being treated differently, or email addresses that changed during the migration). When the match fails, TaskFlow probably writes a session cookie indicating an unauthenticated or invalid state, then redirects back to the SSO provider, creating the loop. The incognito-window success and temporary cookie-clearing fix confirm that stale/conflicting session state is involved, but the loop's return proves the app is actively re-creating the bad state on each auth callback — not just carrying it over from the Okta era.","reproduction_steps":["1. Identify a user with a plus-sign email address (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or one whose primary email was remapped during the Okta-to-Entra migration","2. Have the user clear all cookies for the TaskFlow domain and login.microsoftonline.com","3. Navigate to TaskFlow and click Login","4. Complete authentication at Entra ID (authentication succeeds)","5. Observe redirect back to TaskFlow, then immediate redirect back to Entra ID in a loop","6. Alternatively, log in successfully (may work once after clearing cookies), close the browser, reopen, and navigate to TaskFlow — loop should recur"],"environment":"TaskFlow web application with OIDC/SSO authentication. Recently migrated from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Affects Chrome, Edge, and Firefox equally. Some users have plus-sign email addresses for notification filtering. Some users had primary emails remapped during the Entra ID migration.","severity":"high","impact":"Approximately 30% of the team (~7-9 users) cannot reliably log into TaskFlow. There is no durable workaround — clearing cookies provides only temporary relief. This blocks daily work for affected users.","recommended_fix":"1. Inspect TaskFlow's OIDC callback handler: examine how it matches the incoming identity token (email/sub claim) to existing user records. Check whether email comparison is exact-match or if it normalizes plus-sign aliases and case. 2. Compare the email/sub claims in Entra ID tokens for affected vs. unaffected users against what TaskFlow has stored in its user table. 3. Check the session/cookie logic in the callback: when the user match fails, does it set a cookie that triggers re-authentication rather than showing an error? This silent-failure-to-redirect pattern is the likely loop mechanism. 4. Fix the email matching to handle plus-sign normalization (strip the +tag portion before comparison) and account for email address changes from the migration. 5. Consider adding a loop-detection mechanism (e.g., count redirects within a time window) that surfaces an error message instead of looping indefinitely.","proposed_test_case":"Create test users with these email variants: (a) standard email matching Entra and TaskFlow exactly, (b) plus-sign email like user+tag@domain.com, (c) email that differs between Entra primary email and TaskFlow stored email. Verify that the OIDC callback successfully matches and authenticates all three variants without redirect loops. Additionally, test that after a session expires or browser restart, re-authentication succeeds without requiring cookie clearing.","information_gaps":["Exact email comparison logic in TaskFlow's OIDC callback handler (requires code inspection)","Precise contents of the OIDC token claims (email, sub, preferred_username) for affected vs. unaffected users","Whether TaskFlow uses a session cookie, JWT in localStorage, or another mechanism for post-auth session persistence","Full audit of which affected users have plus-sign emails vs. remapped emails vs. both — reporter estimated from memory","Whether the 1-2 plus-sign users who are NOT affected have any distinguishing configuration"]}
